name: Central-West
bestTimeToVisit: Visit between May and September during the dry season for the best wildlife and landscape viewing.

places.name: Brasília
places.description: Brazil's modernist capital city designed by Oscar Niemeyer, featuring iconic architecture like the Cathedral and Congress buildings. A UNESCO World Heritage site essential for understanding contemporary Brazilian design and political history.

activities.detail: Oscar Niemeyer's stunning modernist church featuring a hyperboloid structure with 16 concrete columns and soaring interior flooded with natural light.
The iconic seat of Brazil's government with its distinctive twin towers and dome, offering guided tours through the legislative chambers and halls of power.
The presidential residence designed by Niemeyer with elegant modernist columns reflected in surrounding pools, viewable from the exterior grounds.
Houses important artworks and artifacts documenting the city's construction and Brazil's cultural heritage within a striking modernist building.
A massive artificial lake offering scenic waterfront walks, sunset views, and leisure activities surrounding the city's architectural landmarks.
A 224-meter tall observation tower providing panoramic 360-degree views of Brasília's unique urban layout and surrounding landscape.
An architectural museum documenting the urban planning and design philosophy behind Brasília's creation by master planner Lúcio Costa.

food.detail: A hot Amazonian soup made with tucupi broth, shrimp, jambu leaves, and gum, traditionally served in a gourd and found in street food stalls throughout the city.
A creamy cassava root soup enriched with cheese and butter, reflecting Brasília's Central-West culinary traditions.
Rice cooked with pequi fruit, a regional specialty from Central Brazil with a distinctive nutty flavor and creamy texture.
Fresh sugarcane juice served ice-cold, a refreshing street drink perfect for Brasília's tropical climate and commonly found at food kiosks.
A savory pie from Goiás state filled with chicken, hard-boiled eggs, and olives, representing local comfort food culture.
A traditional cornmeal cake that is moist and slightly sweet, frequently served as a dessert or afternoon snack in local cafés.

culture.detail: When visiting government buildings or attending cultural events, Brasília residents typically dress formally, reflecting the city's administrative importance and professional atmosphere.
Brasília's addresses follow a unique alphanumeric grid system that can confuse first-time visitors, so downloading maps or asking locals for directions is highly recommended.
Locals take great pride in the city's architectural heritage and UNESCO World Heritage status, so showing genuine interest in Niemeyer's design philosophy will resonate with residents.
Many museums and cultural spaces offer free or discounted entry on weekends, and locals frequently gather at these venues for family outings and cultural engagement.

places.name: Chapada Diamantina
places.description: A stunning plateau region featuring dramatic waterfalls, valleys, and hiking trails in one of Brazil's most scenic landscapes. The national park offers adventure activities and encounters with colonial mining towns.

activities.detail: Hike to Brazil's highest waterfall at 380 meters, located in the Vale do Capão valley with stunning views of the surrounding plateau.
Swim in this enchanting blue underground pool fed by natural springs, located near the town of Itaetê with dramatic cave formations.
Trek to this iconic viewpoint above Lençóis to witness the sunrise over the plateau and valleys below.
Explore this extensive limestone cave system with underground lakes and passages that showcase the region's geological wonders.
Walk through this bohemian valley settlement surrounded by waterfalls, known for its alternative community and natural beauty.
Enjoy a refreshing swim in this natural pool surrounded by lush vegetation near the town of Lençóis.
Stroll through the historic streets of Lençóis, a former diamond mining center with colonial architecture, local shops, and restaurants.
Visit this stunning sinkhole cave with crystal-clear water perfect for swimming and exploring underground passages.

food.detail: A traditional cornmeal cake that is a regional specialty, often served at local bakeries and cafés throughout the plateau towns.
A hearty Northeastern dish of rice and beans cooked together with dried meat and served as a local comfort food staple.
Roasted breadfruit served with local honey, a simple but delicious dessert reflecting the region's natural bounty.
Sun-dried beef prepared in traditional Northeastern style, commonly served with cassava flour and beans at local restaurants.
Fried plantain slices served as a side dish or snack, often eaten with cheese or enjoyed alongside main meals.

culture.detail: Mountain guides from Lençóis and Vale do Capão are essential for navigating trails safely and learning about the region's geology, ecology, and history.
The region's identity is tied to its diamond mining past—explore museums and colonial sites to understand this crucial historical chapter respectfully.
The local culture values connection to nature and community; plan for multi-day stays in smaller towns to appreciate the relaxed pace and build relationships with residents.
Purchase crafts, foods, and services directly from local makers and guides to contribute meaningfully to the communities you visit.

places.name: Goiânia
places.description: Another planned city with Art Deco architecture and green spaces, serving as the capital of Goiás state. Known for its cultural scene, museums, and vibrant nightlife.

activities.detail: The heart of Goiânia's planned center, this massive Art Deco square features government buildings, museums, and green spaces perfect for strolling and people-watching.
Located in the Praça Cívica area, this art museum showcases contemporary Brazilian and local Goiás artists in a striking modernist building.
A 40-hectare nature preserve with native buriti palm trees, walking trails, and a small zoo featuring regional wildlife.
The historic residence of Goiânia's founder, this museum displays period furniture and documents chronicling the city's creation and early development.
A vibrant street market in the downtown area where locals gather for street food, handmade crafts, and live music on weekends.
An urban park with sports facilities, gardens, and cultural venues that hosts outdoor concerts and events throughout the year.

food.detail: A regional specialty combining rice with pequi fruit, a local ingredient with a distinctive nutty and slightly aromatic flavor unique to Central-West Brazil.
A traditional cornmeal cake that is soft, slightly sweet, and commonly served as a dessert or breakfast item at local bakeries and street vendors.
A savory corn paste wrapped in corn husks and boiled, often served with melted cheese and eaten as a quick snack or light meal.
A hearty fish broth made with piranha caught from local rivers, served as a traditional appetizer or light course at regional restaurants.
The classic Brazilian combination of guava paste served alongside fresh local cheese, offering a perfect balance of sweetness and creaminess.

culture.detail: Goiânia's geometric grid and wide avenues were designed by Lúcio Costa; walking or cycling along the orderly streets reveals the city's unique Art Deco planning philosophy.
The city hosts vibrant music and theater festivals year-round; check local calendars as many events celebrate both Brazilian modernism and Central-West regional traditions.
Goiânia's reputation for active nightlife is centered in specific neighborhoods like Marista and Centro; visitors should stick to well-lit, populated areas and travel with others after dark.

culture.text: Learn about the city's founding story
culture.detail: Goiânia was purpose-built in 1933 as part of Getúlio Vargas's modernization campaign; locals take pride in this planned urban design and appreciate visitors' interest in the city's architectural heritage.

places.name: Pirenópolis
places.description: A charming colonial town surrounded by waterfalls and natural pools in the Pireneus Mountains. Popular for adventure sports, spa treatments, and enjoying well-preserved historical architecture.

activities.detail: A stunning 30-meter waterfall with a natural pool perfect for swimming and diving into crystalline waters surrounded by lush forest.
A picturesque waterfall accessible via a scenic hiking trail that features a natural stone bridge formation and multiple cascading pools.

activities.text: Parque Estadual da Serra dos Pireneus
activities.detail: A protected natural area offering hiking trails with panoramic views of the Pireneus Mountains and access to diverse flora and fauna.
Explore beautifully preserved colonial architecture, baroque churches, and cobblestone streets lined with art galleries, shops, and cafes in the heart of town.
An exhilarating canopy adventure through the forest with multiple zip-line courses offering aerial views of the surrounding landscape.
Relax at local spas offering natural therapies using regional plants and mineral-rich waters inspired by the area's natural resources.

activities.text: Matriz Church (Igreja Matriz de Nossa Senhora do Rosário)
activities.detail: An 18th-century baroque church featuring intricate artwork, historical significance, and beautiful architectural details in the town's central plaza.

food.detail: A unique regional fruit with a large pit and creamy pulp used in traditional stews, rice dishes, and as a flavor-defining ingredient in Goiás cuisine.
A hearty baked pastry filled with chicken, cheese, and spices that represents local comfort food and can be found at bakeries throughout town.
Fresh-caught trout prepared simply with local herbs and served at restaurants, taking advantage of the cool mountain streams surrounding the town.
A traditional cornbread made with local corn flour, often served warm at cafes and as a staple accompaniment to regional meals.
A classic pairing of creamy regional cheese with guava paste, reflecting the culinary heritage of Central-West Brazil and commonly served as dessert.

culture.detail: Pirenópolis hosts an annual film festival typically in August that attracts visitors and celebrates both Brazilian and international cinema culture.
A deeply rooted religious celebration honoring the Holy Spirit with processions, street parties, and traditional foods, reflecting the town's spiritual heritage.
Visitors should practice Leave No Trace principles when visiting waterfalls and natural areas, as the community takes preservation of these sacred natural spaces seriously.
The town has a thriving community of local artists, craftspeople, and artisans whose work is displayed in galleries and studios throughout the historic center.

places.name: Cuiabá
places.description: The capital of Mato Grosso state and gateway to the Pantanal wetlands, one of the world's largest tropical wetland areas. Essential as a hub for wildlife viewing and experiencing Brazil's interior culture.

activities.detail: Multi-day guided expeditions departing from Cuiabá into the world's largest tropical wetland to spot jaguars, caimans, capybaras, and over 650 bird species.
A scenic plateau park 50km northeast of Cuiabá featuring dramatic waterfalls, canyons, and hiking trails through cerrado vegetation.
The main river port where travelers board boats for Pantanal expeditions, offering a glimpse of local commerce and transport culture.
A compact museum in central Cuiabá displaying artifacts, tools, and cultural items from regional indigenous peoples like the Bororo and Karajá.
The striking white neoclassical cathedral in Praça Alencastro is an iconic landmark and religious center in the city's historic core.
Strolls along the riverfront and through traditional neighborhoods reveal colonial architecture and local life in Brazil's interior.

food.detail: A regional specialty from local rivers featuring piranha fish slow-cooked in broth with vegetables and served with rice and farofa.
A large freshwater fish native to Pantanal rivers grilled simply with lime and served as a favorite protein in local restaurants.
A hearty morning soup or breakfast dish made with beef stock, vegetables, and sometimes tucupi (cassava broth), reflecting cattle-ranching culture.
A traditional sweet cornmeal cake often enjoyed as a snack or dessert, reflecting interior Brazilian comfort food traditions.
Natural guaraná drinks and açai bowls sold at local markets and praças, offering an authentic taste of Amazonian ingredients.

culture.detail: Cuiabá residents and guides take environmental conservation seriously; always follow local advice on safe water access and animal encounters in the Pantanal.
Most Pantanal tours and activities depart at dawn; locals expect early starts and value punctuality for river and wildlife expeditions.
While Portuguese is spoken, the interior accent and vocabulary differ from coastal Brazil; patience and basic Portuguese phrases are helpful when interacting with locals.
Dry season (May to September) offers better wildlife viewing and accessibility; wet season (November to March) can cause road and water route closures, so plan accordingly.

places.name: Pantanal
places.description: The world's largest tropical wetland spanning three countries, home to jaguars, caimans, giant otters, and over 650 bird species. A wildlife paradise best experienced through guided lodge stays and boat tours.

activities.detail: Search for jaguars along riverbanks during guided boat tours, particularly in the Northern Pantanal where sightings are most frequent.
Navigate narrow channels at dusk in small boats to spot caimans with headlamps reflecting their eyes, a thrilling nocturnal wildlife experience.
Hike through flooded forests and wetland trails to observe these playful aquatic mammals in their natural habitat, often found in family groups.
Drive or ride along the famous dirt highway through the wetland to spot over 650 bird species including macaws, herons, and jabirus.
Explore the seasonal pastures and wetland edges on horseback, a traditional way ranch hands navigate the terrain during dry season.
Join guided fishing excursions to safely catch piranhas, which are then often prepared fresh at lodge kitchens for dinner.
Paddle through narrow water channels at dawn to experience the wetland awakening with minimal disturbance to wildlife.

food.detail: Dried beef cooked with rice, a hearty ranch staple that fuels local workers and provides authentic cowboy cuisine.
Grilled freshwater pacu fish caught locally, served simply with lime and farinha de mandioca (cassava flour) for authentic riverside dining.
A traditional soup made from freshly caught piranhas with vegetables and spices, offering a unique taste of the wetland's most famous fish.
Brazilian barbecue featuring local beef grilled over open fires, reflecting the region's cattle ranching heritage and cowboy culture.
The Amazonian superfood bowl served with granola and fresh fruits, increasingly popular at eco-lodges as a healthy breakfast option.

culture.detail: Plan visits during dry season (May-September) when wildlife congregates and roads are passable; wet season floods make access difficult.
Always hire certified local guides for safety and conservation; they follow strict protocols to minimize disturbance to animals and wetland ecosystems.
The Pantanal was built on cattle ranching tradition; many lodges operate as working fazendas where you may encounter vaqueiros (cowboys) maintaining the land.

places.name: Caldas Novas
places.description: Brazil's largest hot spring resort town featuring thermal water parks and spas set in lush countryside. Popular for relaxation, wellness treatments, and family entertainment.
imageQueries: Caldas Novas hot springs thermal water

activities.detail: The largest water park in South America featuring natural thermal pools, slides, and wave pools heated by geothermal springs.
Indulge in massages, mud wraps, and hydrotherapy using mineral-rich thermal waters from the region's natural springs.
Explore multiple open-air thermal swimming areas set within lush gardens where visitors can relax in naturally heated waters of varying temperatures.
Trek through countryside to this scenic waterfall located in the surrounding hills, offering a refreshing natural pool for swimming.
Climb to this hilltop lookout point for panoramic views of the thermal resort town and surrounding cerrado landscape.
Browse handcrafted goods, gemstones, and local products at weekend markets and shops throughout the town center.

food.detail: Locally-caught fresh trout prepared with almond sauce, reflecting the region's freshwater fishing traditions.
A traditional cornmeal cake that is a beloved local specialty often served with coffee or as a dessert in regional restaurants.
Grilled peacock bass, a popular freshwater fish from nearby rivers, seasoned simply to highlight its delicate flavor.
A hearty fish stew combining local river fish with vegetables and broth, representing Goiás regional comfort food.
Refreshing drinks made with the region's famous mineral water combined with local tropical fruits like passion fruit and açaí.

culture.detail: Respect posted temperature zones in pools and thermal areas, as some springs reach extremely high temperatures; always test water before entering.
Book accommodations and treatments well in advance during Brazilian school holidays (July, December-January) when the resort fills with families seeking wellness breaks.
The town's identity centers on health and relaxation; expect a slower pace and rhythm aligned with spa and thermal bathing schedules rather than nightlife tourism.
Residents speak Brazilian Portuguese with a Central-West accent; English is limited outside major resort hotels, so basic Portuguese phrases are helpful.

places.name: Campo Grande
places.description: The capital of Mato Grosso do Sul state, serving as another access point to the Pantanal and showcasing regional cattle ranching culture. Known for its rodeos, museums, and agricultural heritage.
imageQueries: Campo Grande city Mato Grosso do Sul

activities.detail: This museum showcases artifacts and exhibits of indigenous peoples from Mato Grosso do Sul, including pottery, tools, and traditional crafts.
A large urban park featuring indigenous sculptures, walking trails, and a lake, offering insight into regional native cultures and natural landscapes.
The main market in the city center where locals shop for regional produce, crafts, and regional products reflecting agricultural heritage.
Seasonal rodeo events showcasing traditional cattle ranching competitions, bull riding, and regional cowboy culture central to Mato Grosso do Sul.
Contemporary art museum housed in a historic building displaying works by regional and Brazilian artists.

activities.text: Secretaria de Estado de Meio Ambiente (Pantanal Visitor Center)
activities.detail: Information and exhibition center providing context about the nearby Pantanal wetlands and opportunities to arrange eco-tours into the biome.

food.detail: Regional barbecued beef prepared with salt and minimal seasoning, showcasing the quality of local cattle and ranching traditions.
A baked fish dish made with salted pirarucu (giant Amazon fish), potatoes, and palm oil, reflecting Pantanal and river culture.
Cornmeal cake, a traditional regional dessert often served at fairs and local festivities with a dense, sweet texture.
A soup made from piranha fish caught in local rivers, traditionally consumed as a hearty starter in regional restaurants.

culture.detail: Campo Grande hosts major rodeos throughout the year where locals gather in cowboy attire; attending shows respect for regional identity and cattle-ranching heritage.
The region is home to several indigenous groups; many museums and cultural sites educate visitors about their ongoing presence and historical significance in Mato Grosso do Sul.
The city calendar revolves around agricultural fairs and cattle auctions; timing a visit during Expoagro or similar events provides authentic cultural experiences.

places.name: Poços de Caldas
places.description: A historic thermal spa town nestled in a volcanic crater, combining belle époque architecture with natural hot springs and mountain scenery. Ideal for health-conscious travelers and those seeking relaxation.
imageQueries: Poços de Caldas thermal spa architecture

activities.text: Thermal Spa at Parque José Affonso Junqueira
activities.detail: Soak in naturally heated mineral-rich pools within the main park, surrounded by belle époque pavilions and manicured gardens.
Trek to the Cristo Redentor statue perched on the volcanic crater rim for panoramic views of the town and surrounding mountains.
Explore this historic thermal fountain nestled in a scenic grotto, originally built during the spa town's heyday in the early 20th century.

activities.text: Lake Poços de Caldas Scenic Walk
activities.detail: Stroll around the artificial lake in the volcanic crater, enjoying mountain vistas and visiting Belle Époque-era gazebos and pavilions.
Indulge in therapeutic mud treatments and massage therapies at one of the town's historic spas using local thermal mud.
Browse and purchase handcrafted opal jewelry and gemstones from local artisans, as Poços de Caldas is known for its precious stone industry.

food.detail: A local sponge cake with coconut, often enjoyed with afternoon coffee at traditional tea houses throughout town.
Fresh grilled or poached trout caught in the cool mountain waters surrounding Poços de Caldas, served at family-run restaurants.

food.text: Goiabada com Queijo (Guava Paste with Cheese)
food.detail: A traditional pairing of sweet guava paste with local fresh cheese, perfect as an afternoon snack or dessert.
A hearty regional stew featuring beans, pork, and locally foraged vegetables, reflecting Minas Gerais culinary traditions.
Local thermal water is bottled and sometimes infused with local herbs and fruits, believed to have healing properties.

culture.detail: Respect posted water temperatures and designated spa zones, as water varies in heat intensity throughout the park; bathing suits are required in public areas.
The town takes pride in its historic architecture and early 1900s grandeur; visitors are encouraged to appreciate and photograph the well-preserved buildings and monuments.
Many locals and repeat visitors come specifically for wellness and healing; the town has a relaxed, spa-like atmosphere where afternoon siestas and slow-paced activities are the norm.
Winter months (June–August) draw the largest crowds seeking thermal baths for warmth, so plan visits during shoulder seasons for a more peaceful experience.

places.name: Palmas
places.description: The modern capital of Tocantins state, built on the banks of the Tocantins River with planned urban design and a relaxed atmosphere. Known for water sports, beautiful sunsets, and proximity to natural attractions.

activities.detail: A river beach along the Tocantins River where locals swim, sunbathe, and watch stunning sunsets, especially popular on weekends.
A waterfront park with walking trails, picnic areas, and views of the river, ideal for morning jogs or evening relaxation.

activities.text: Jet ski and water sports on Tocantins River
activities.detail: Rent jet skis, paddleboards, or take boat tours along the river to experience Palmas' primary recreational resource.
The state government palace showcasing modernist architecture with guided tours available that explain Palmas' planned urban development.
An artificial lagoon in the city center surrounded by green space, perfect for birdwatching and evening walks.
A natural area about 30 kilometers from Palmas featuring waterfalls, swimming holes, and hiking trails through cerrado landscape.

food.detail: A traditional Amazonian dish of shredded pirarucu fish mixed with cassava flour, onions, and spices, baked until crispy.
A hot, sour soup made with cassava broth, dried shrimp, and tucupi (cassava juice), served in a gourd and eaten with a wooden spoon.
Rice cooked with pequi fruit, a native Tocantins ingredient with a distinctive aromatic flavor and creamy texture.
Grilled river fish (peacock bass) seasoned simply with lime and salt, reflecting the local preference for fresh river catches.
A sweet cornmeal cake popular at local cafes and street vendors, often served with coffee or fresh juice.

culture.detail: Palmas operates on a slower, more laid-back schedule than major Brazilian cities, so plan activities without rushing and expect longer lunch hours.
The Tocantins River is central to daily life and recreation; locals take water activities seriously, so follow safety guidelines and local advice when swimming or boating.
May through September offers the best weather and most accessible activities, while October to April brings heavy rains that can affect river conditions and outdoor plans.
Palmas is one of Brazil's newest capital cities (founded 1989) with a grid layout; understanding its modern, organized structure helps navigation and appreciation of urban planning.

places.name: Cidade de Goiás
places.description: A UNESCO World Heritage colonial town featuring well-preserved 18th-century architecture and culture, hosting traditional celebrations like Holy Week processions. A window into Brazil's mining era heritage.
imageQueries: Cidade de Goiás colonial architecture UNESCO

activities.text: Basilica of Our Lady of Abadia
activities.detail: Marvel at the 18th-century baroque church with ornate gilt interiors and explore the adjacent museum displaying religious artifacts and colonial-era vestments.
Witness centuries-old penitential processions during Semana Santa featuring hooded participants carrying crosses through candlelit colonial streets.
Tour the childhood home and museum dedicated to the renowned local poet and writer, preserving her literary legacy and personal belongings.
Stroll through the UNESCO-protected town center admiring preserved 18th-century colonial architecture with whitewashed walls, wooden shutters, and cobblestone plazas.
Hike to this natural waterfall located near the town for swimming and picnicking among surrounding vegetation.
Explore artifacts and exhibits documenting the 18th-century gold mining boom that founded and enriched Cidade de Goiás.

food.detail: A dense, savory cornbread sweetened slightly and studded with local goat cheese, served warm at bakeries throughout the town.
A traditional regional dish combining fresh river fish with local vegetables and cassava root, reflecting the area's culinary heritage.
Rice cooked with the native pequi fruit that imparts a distinctive nutty, aromatic flavor unique to Central-West Brazil.
Sample local candies and paçoca (peanut brittle) made with traditional recipes passed through generations of local confectioners.
Tour artisanal cachaça producers in and around town to taste sugarcane spirit infused with native fruits and herbs.

culture.detail: If visiting during Semana Santa, expect crowded streets and closed businesses as residents participate in processions; book accommodations well in advance.
Many activities center on the baroque basilica and churches; dress modestly and be respectful during active religious ceremonies.
The town's identity is deeply rooted in 18th-century gold mining and colonial heritage, so visiting museums and guides provides context for understanding preserved architecture.
Cidade de Goiás is a small, walkable town designed for leisurely exploration; allow multiple days to appreciate colonial details and connect with locals at cafés.

places.name: Bonito
places.description: An eco-tourism paradise featuring crystal-clear rivers, cenotes, and waterfalls perfect for snorkeling, diving, and cave exploration. Known for exceptional water clarity and conservation efforts.

activities.detail: Float downstream through crystalline waters observing tropical fish and submerged rock formations in this world-renowned snorkeling river.
Descend into a massive flooded sinkhole filled with freshwater to explore pristine cave systems and unique aquatic ecosystems.
Visit a stunning cave featuring an underground lake with brilliant blue waters accessed by natural stone formations.
Enjoy the local natural spring-fed pool maintained as a municipal swimming area with clear, refreshing waters.

activities.text: Waterfall rappelling at Cachoeira da Formiga
activities.detail: Descend waterfalls by rope while surrounded by lush vegetation and pristine forest ecosystems.
Navigate scenic sections of this emerald river while spotting wildlife and learning about local conservation efforts.

food.detail: Grilled pacu fish from local rivers seasoned simply with salt and lime, highlighting the region's fresh freshwater catch.
Deep-fried cassava root served as a crispy side dish or snack, a staple throughout Central-West Brazil.
Rice cooked with pequi fruit, a regional delicacy that provides a unique nutty and aromatic flavor indigenous to the Cerrado.
Fresh regional fish prepared in the Pantanal style with local herbs and often served with rice and beans.
Sweet corn cake made with fresh corn during harvest season, a traditional dessert throughout the region.

culture.detail: Many natural areas in Bonito require licensed guides and have visitor limits; always follow these regulations to protect the delicate ecosystems.
Locals appreciate visitors who learn basic Portuguese phrases like 'Olá' and 'Obrigado,' as English is limited outside tourist areas.
Supporting local guide services directly benefits the community and provides authentic knowledge about conservation and regional ecology.
Never use sunscreen in rivers and cenotes as it damages sensitive aquatic life; guides will provide biodegradable alternatives.

restaurants.name: Casa de Pasta
restaurants.description: Family-run Italian restaurant serving handmade pasta and traditional sauces in a warm, welcoming atmosphere.

restaurants.name: Flor do Cerrado
restaurants.description: Contemporary take on Goiás regional cuisine featuring cerrado ingredients and traditional recipes reimagined.

restaurants.name: Churrascaria Pedra Grande
restaurants.description: Premium rodízio restaurant with excellent cuts of meat and comprehensive wine selection in upscale surroundings.

restaurants.name: Restaurante do Gilson
restaurants.description: Casual spot specializing in authentic Mato Grosso dishes with generous portions and friendly service.

restaurants.name: Natureza Pura
restaurants.description: Health-conscious restaurant with organic ingredients, creative vegetarian dishes, and fresh juices.

restaurants.name: Saíba Gourmet
restaurants.description: Elegant restaurant in a colonial setting with creative local cuisine and artistic presentation.
restaurants.mustOrder: Tropeiro de Cogumelos com Queijo da Serra

restaurants.name: Bar do Brito
restaurants.description: Laid-back bar and restaurant with traditional food, cold beer, and views of the historic town square.

restaurants.name: Tablado Steakhouse
restaurants.description: High-end steakhouse with prime cuts, sommelier service, and sophisticated ambiance in the federal capital.

restaurants.name: Beiragen
restaurants.description: Classic Portuguese restaurant serving authentic bacalau, seafood, and traditional recipes with attentive service.

restaurants.name: Casa Grande
restaurants.description: Family restaurant celebrating Goiás culinary traditions with hearty portions and warm hospitality.

restaurants.name: Nativa Espresso
restaurants.description: Modern café with excellent coffee, pastries, and light lunches; popular with locals and travelers.

restaurants.name: Fazenda do Cerrado
restaurants.description: Rural restaurant on working farm showcasing regional meat preparations and cerrado ingredients in rustic setting.

restaurants.name: Casa del Gusto
restaurants.description: Charming restaurant near the Vale da Lua with Italian influences and locally-sourced ingredients.
restaurants.mustOrder: Rigatoni com Molho de Cogumelos Selvagens

restaurants.name: O Sertanejo
restaurants.description: Casual restaurant serving regional Goiás cuisine near the famous hot springs, ideal for post-spa dining.
restaurants.mustOrder: Caldo de Piranha com Mandioca Frita
